summary: On Thursday, the Supreme Court ruled 6-3 that the Trump administration can terminate Temporary Protected Status for Haiti and Syria, overturning lower court injunctions blocking the action.

Created by Congress in 1990, the program protects 1.3 million immigrants from 17 countries; the Trump administration has sought to end designations for 11 additional nations including Afghanistan and Venezuela.

For local communities, the ruling threatens work permits for about 15,000 Haitians in Springfield and 4,000 in Wicomico County, with leaders warning of severe disruption to health care, hospitality, and poultry processing.
Gov. Ron DeSantis called the ruling a "correct decision," while U.S. Rep. Jared Moskowitz warned of "cascading economic consequences," and Biassu Pierre said the news was "overwhelming."

Writing for the majority, Justice Samuel Alito asserted federal law allows "no judicial review" of Homeland Security's termination decisions, while Justice Elena Kagan dissented, arguing plaintiffs deserve relief while litigating constitutional claims.
title: Source Icon

bias: Left coverage frames the ruling as another expansion of Second Amendment power, stressing dissent and state limits on gun control, while right coverage casts it as a “major win,” using combative, celebratory language like “vampire,” “shoots down,” and “another one bites the dust” to portray Hawaii ’s rule as an illegitimate barrier.

The key dividing line is the law’s “permission” requirement: left and center treat it as a legal restriction, but the right turns it into a symbol of anti-gun overreach, even invoking the Fourteenth Amendment and broader culture-war stakes.
Both sides note the 6-3 decision and its reach into stores, hotels, and malls.
summary: The Supreme Court ruled 6-3 on Thursday that Hawaii's law prohibiting concealed carry on private property without express permission violates the Second Amendment, effectively blocking enforcement of the statute.
Hawaii enacted Act 52 in 2023, following the Supreme Court's 2022 Bruen decision, to regulate firearms on private property open to the public.
Writing for the majority, Justice Samuel Alito said the measure "severely hampers" citizens' ability to carry weapons in everyday places like stores and restaurants.

Supreme Court Justice Ketanji Brown Jackson , joined by Justice Sonia Sotomayor, warned the ruling prioritizes "protecting guns" over legal principles, threatening similar policies in New York, California, New Jersey, and Maryland.

The ruling likely shifts the default rule nationwide to permit carry in businesses unless owners explicitly prohibit it, illustrating a central tension between property rights and firearm access shaping Second Amendment law.
title: Supreme Court strikes down Hawaii’s private property gun restrictions

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the ruling as a blow to asylum rights, stressing humanitarian harm with words like “restrictive,” “by force,” and “dire dissent,” and spotlighting Sotomayor’s warning that people could be blocked from filing claims.
Right-Leaning coverage casts the same 6-3 decision as a “major immigration victory” for Trump, leaning on “victories,” “turn away,” and “border curbs” to celebrate enforcement and legality.

Center coverage stays more procedural, using “clears the way” and “siding with Trump.” All sides agree on the metering policy and “arrived” question, but they split over whether the ruling protects borders or endangers people.
summary: In a 6-3 decision on Thursday, the Supreme Court ruled that federal law does not require the government to process asylum claims from migrants barred from setting foot on United States soil at ports of entry.
Known as 'metering,' the policy limiting daily asylum claims was used by Democratic and Republican administrations before the Biden administration rescinded it in 2021.
The Justice Department attorney Vivek Suri argued before the Supreme Court that migrants cannot 'arrive in' the United States while standing in Mexico, contradicting a 2024 Circuit Court ruling.

Lawyers for asylum seekers Mullin and Otro Lado challenged the practice, arguing that 'vulnerable families, children, and adults fleeing persecution were stranded in perilous conditions where they faced violent assault, kidnapping, and death.'
The Trump administration views metering as a 'critical tool' for addressing border surges, positioning Border Protection to potentially reinstate the practice following the Supreme Court's decision.
title: Supreme Court rules asylum seekers can be turned back at US border

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the ruling as a corporate shield for Bayer /Monsanto, stressing “blocks,” “shields,” and the loss of recourse for plaintiffs injured by “toxic” Roundup, often amplifying Justice Jackson’s “remarkable and regrettable” dissent and the human stakes of cancer claims.

Right-Leaning coverage instead casts it as a clean victory, using sharper, more combative language like “sides with,” “guts,” “alleged cancer risk,” and “MAHA” fallout, while highlighting farmers, agriculture, and stock gains as proof of a broader win.
Both agree the 7-2 decision turns on federal preemption and shuts down thousands of suits, but they diverge on whether that means accountability denied or legal clarity restored.
summary: The U.S. Supreme Court ruled 7–2 on Thursday in favor of Monsanto , dealing a massive legal victory to the agrochemical giant by heavily restricting the ability of cancer patients to sue over the weedkiller Roundup.

The landmark decision establishes that federal regulation overrides state law, with Justice Brett Kavanaugh writing for the majority that because the EPA evaluated glyphosate and explicitly determined it does not require a cancer warning, state-level "failure to warn" lawsuits are legally preempted.

The ruling severely handicaps a wave of roughly 200,000 product liability claims, shutting the door on tens of thousands of pending lawsuits filed by home users and groundskeepers who allege they developed non-Hodgkin lymphoma from the product.

The case centered on Missouri resident John Durnell, who was awarded $1.25 million by a state jury in 2023 after using Roundup for over two decades to beautify local parks; Thursday’s high court decision effectively invalidates those state court findings.

The decision creates a complex political rift for the Trump administration, as the ruling aligns with the administration's business deregulation goals but severely frustrates Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. and the "Make America Healthy Again" movement, which has fiercely campaigned to outlaw glyphosate.
title: Supreme Court shuts down thousands of lawsuits that claim Roundup weedkiller might cause cancer

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the ruling as a rebuke to a broader Trump election agenda, using loaded terms like “crackdown,” “ voter suppression,” and “election meddling” to portray the order as an attack on ballot access.

Right-Leaning coverage instead spotlights “election integrity” and “tightening rules,” while partisan cues like “Obama judge,” “JUST IN,” and “defeat for Trump” cast the decision as ideologically tilted interference.
The key dividing line is motive: one side emphasizes suppressive intent, the other fraud-prevention aims and state-federal power.
Both agree on the court block and appeal, but diverge sharply on what it means for democracy.
summary: On Thursday, District Judge Indira Talwani blocked parts of President Donald Trump 's executive order on mail-in voting, preventing it from taking force ahead of elections that will decide control of Congress.

President Trump signed the order on March 31, citing election fraud claims to justify restricting mail-in voting and directing the Department of Justice to prioritize prosecution of officials issuing ballots to people deemed "not eligible" to vote.

Postmaster General David Steiner told lawmakers Wednesday that the Postal Service would refuse to deliver ballots if states decline to share absentee voter lists, as USPS recently moved to implement these new ballot rules.

Democratic-Led states challenged the directive, arguing it unlawfully interferes with election administration and risks disenfranchising eligible voters; Judge Talwani sided with the states, preventing the order's enforcement for now.

The Trump administration is expected to appeal, while Democrats contest a separate ruling by Trump-appointed District Judge Carl Nichols in Washington. That case is moving to the Appeals Court for the District of Columbia.
title: US judge blocks Trump's mail-in voting executive order

summary: On Thursday, Iran 's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps attacked a Singapore-flagged cargo ship in the Strait of Hormuz, damaging the vessel's bridge near Oman, United States officials confirmed.
The attack occurred hours after Tehran warned vessels to avoid unauthorized routes, undermining a United States-Iran agreement signed last week that mandates keeping the strategic waterway open.

Following the projectile strike, the International Maritime Organization paused its evacuation operation for stranded ships, with Secretary-General Arsenio Dominguez stating "seafarer safety remains paramount."
South Korea's Oceans Ministry reported that additional vessels exited the Strait of Hormuz, leaving five South Korean-operated ships in the area with 47 crew members on board.

Roughly 20% of the world's oil and gas passed through the Strait of Hormuz before this spring's regional conflict, making security in the passage critical for international trade amid price volatility.
title: UN agency says it pauses Hormuz ship evacuation initiative after vessel attacked

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the White House request as restraint and skepticism, stressing “limit,” “request,” and even the sarcastic “voluntary review” angle, while the center softens that into a safety-led “slow roll” and adds the Anthropic export-control backdrop.

By contrast, the right shifts away from U.S. regulation entirely and spotlights Chinese AI momentum, using upbeat, competitive language like “beats,” “narrows the gap,” and “targets AGI” to suggest U.S. leaders are losing distance.

The key dividing line is whether the story is about oversight versus rivalry; both sides still converge on one theme: advanced AI is powerful enough to trigger government pressure and strategic concern.
summary: The White House requested OpenAI limit the release of its upcoming GPT-5.6 model to a small number of government-approved partners, citing concerns over its advanced cybersecurity capabilities.

Shifting from its initial 'hands off' stance, the Trump administration under President Donald Trump has pushed for federal oversight of new models in recent months, including an executive order earlier this month requiring voluntary submissions for government review 30 days before release.

In a staff memo on Thursday, OpenAI CEO Sam Altman told employees the government is "approving access customer by customer during this preview period," with plans for a broader release a "couple of weeks later."

Brad Carson, head of Public First, a bipartisan pro-AI safety super PAC, characterized current federal oversight as an "ad hoc, personalized, opaque, possibly lawless approach" lacking transparency and basic fairness. Experts warn confusion persists over which agency directs AI regulation.

Earlier this year, Anthropic restricted its Claude Mythos model through Project Glasswing, illustrating the industry's struggle navigating a "strange moment" without a formal federal regulatory framework. The government is expected to create standardized assessment criteria for future releases.
title: White House asks OpenAI to limit its next model release

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the rulings through human consequences—“America’s immigrants,” Haitians and Syrians, and advocacy language like “condemns,” “devastating,” “cruel and callous,” even “fascist immigration agenda”—while right-leaning coverage centers executive power and policy wins, portraying Trump’s immigration agenda as getting a “boost” and stressing “major rulings” and “future presidents.” The biggest dividing lines are immigration review, gun law, and Monsanto liability: the left highlights harm and accountability, the right highlights authority and broad legal impact.
Both sides agree the decisions were consequential, but they read them through deeper divides over borders, guns, and state power.
summary: On Thursday, the Supreme Court handed President Donald Trump victories in two immigration cases, allowing the administration to end Temporary Protected Status and restrict asylum seekers at the U.S. border.

The court ruled that statutes bar judicial review of termination decisions, protecting a program that shields roughly 330,000 Haitians and nearly 4,000 Syrians from deportation due to dangerous conditions in their home countries.
Justices agreed 6-3 that "metering," a policy allowing border officials to turn back migrants before they physically enter the U.S., complies with federal law.
Separately on Thursday, the court struck down a Hawaii law requiring gun owners to obtain permission before carrying firearms in public, leaving gun rights advocates with a 2-0 sweep this term.

In a 7-2 decision, the court blocked lawsuits against the weedkiller Roundup, citing federal preemption, while major cases on birthright citizenship and transgender athletes remain unresolved as the term nears completion.
title: 5 Takeaways From Supreme Court’s Big Rulings on Immigration, Guns

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Gregg Phillips as a far-right activist whose ouster from a major FEMA role follows “bizarre” behavior and a history of election conspiracy claims, making the removal feel like accountability for a politicized appointment.

Right-Leaning coverage strips away most of that context and presents the story mainly as a reported personnel change, though it still uses mocking language like “teleported” and “ousted” to signal skepticism.
The sharpest dividing line is whether Phillips’ White House-backed placement in December and his unusual claim are treated as a broader ideological scandal or simply a strange exit.
Both sides agree he was pushed out.
summary: On Thursday, the Department of Homeland Security confirmed that FEMA official Gregg Phillips is on leave after being asked to step away from his role due to concerns about how he is publicly perceived.

Podcast appearances featuring outlandish comments triggered scrutiny after CNN reported in March on Phillips' claims that God teleported him to a Waffle House 50 miles away, alongside violent rhetoric and false election fraud allegations.

White House officials urged DHS to remove Phillips from public view earlier this year, with one saying "Everyone's thoughts were, 'What the hell is this? This guy has got to go,'" The Washington Post noted Phillips lacked prominent emergency management experience.

Former Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem appointed Phillips in December despite his inflammatory record, though President Donald Trump later told CNN the teleportation claims sounded strange and promised to investigate the matter.

His departure reflects broader FEMA leadership changes, including official Cameron Hamilton's ouster after objecting to dismantling the agency, though some career FEMA officials had supported Phillips before his leave.
title: FEMA Official Who Claimed He Teleported to Waffle House Ousted From Agency, Sources Say

bias: Left-Leaning coverage casts the ruling as a “major blow” to the “Trump DOJ,” stressing a fight to keep Epstein material “hidden” and framing disclosure as a transparency victory; right-leaning coverage also centers the unsealing, but pivots to the files’ political sting, spotlighting items “related to Trump,” “co-conspirators,” and a “torture video,” while hinting at the judge’s partisan background.
Center-Leaning coverage stays more procedural, focusing on the order to “unredact” or explain redactions.
The dividing lines are secrecy versus accountability, and Trump-linked implications versus legal process, with both sides agreeing the judge compelled more Epstein disclosure under a deadline.
summary: A federal judge ordered the Department of Justice to unredact and release more records related to convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein or provide detailed explanations for redactions.
Representatives raised concerns over an email referencing a "torture video" where the recipient's identity was redacted.
The ruling adds pressure on the Justice Department to provide broader public access to Epstein investigative files while protecting victims' privacy.
title: Judge orders DOJ to produce, unredact sought after Epstein files

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the measure as a needed way to “backfill” healthcare cuts and highlights union persistence, while also stressing intraparty tensions and liberal opposition; right-leaning coverage recasts the same fight as Newsom’s loss, using sharper language like “fails to stop,” “controversial,” “wealth tax,” and even “battle” to signal conflict and skepticism.

The most revealing divide is whether the story is about protecting low-income healthcare or taxing the rich, with the 5% on billionaires and the temporary nature of the levy presented as either pragmatic funding or aggressive redistribution.
Both sides agree it’s headed to voters and tied to California’s broader ideological split.
summary: On Thursday, proponents of a California billionaire tax measure vowed to move forward with their November ballot initiative despite mounting opposition from political forces and business leaders.

The Service Employees International Union Healthcare Workers West proposed a one-time 5% tax on individuals with net worth exceeding $1 billion to generate $100 billion for Medicaid after federal healthcare funding cuts.

Google co-founder Sergey Brin donated $82 million to the Building a Better California committee, part of a coalition that raised more than $118 million from fewer than a dozen donors opposing the tax.

Two 'poison pill' ballot initiatives also qualified for November, including measures barring new personal property taxes, while Democratic Gov. Gavin Newsom continues opposing the primary billionaire tax proposal.

Critics warn the measure could increase California's budget volatility, while supporters argue the 2026 midterm election must prioritize flipping Congress to counter President Trump's final two years in office.
title: California voters will consider a measure in November to raise taxes on billionaires

bias: Left-Leaning coverage stays comparatively restrained, foregrounding the arrest of a former county coroner and the mortuary probe, while right-leaning framing leans into the “brothers” label and “mishandling remains” for “two dozen people,” stressing scandal and victim scale.

Center coverage is the most detailed and condemnatory, highlighting “hidden” or “decomposing bodies,” the concealed door, 125 corpse-abuse counts, and the phrase “complete disregard for the dignity of the deceased.” The key dividing line is whether the story is cast as routine law-enforcement action, a gruesome misconduct scandal, or a broader moral breakdown around death care.
All sides agree the arrests followed a lengthy investigation.
summary: On Thursday, Brian Lee Cotter and Christopher Aaron Cotter, owners of Davis Mortuary in Pueblo, were arrested following a lengthy investigation sparked by last year's discovery of decomposing bodies behind a concealed door.
State inspectors discovered 24 decomposing bodies, cremains, and bones in a concealed room last August, prompting investigation into systemic physical and financial misconduct at the mortuary.
The Cotters face over 150 felony charges, including 125 counts of abuse of a corpse and multiple theft counts stemming from alleged misappropriation of funds from preneed funeral contracts.
While 19 of 24 bodies have been identified, the remaining five undergo forensic analysis; the 10th Judicial District Attorney's Office prepares restitution paperwork for theft victims.
A judge set a $1 million bond for both men, who remain in the Pueblo County Jail; a gag order prohibits law enforcement and prosecutors from publicly discussing the case.
title: Brothers are accused of mishandling remains of two dozen people at Colorado funeral home

bias: Left and center frame the story as a cautious business update about OpenAI “waiting” or “holding off” until 2027, with the center adding the concrete wrinkle of a confidential filing and a $1 trillion valuation tradeoff, while the right turns it into a broader market drama, stressing “rocky debut,” “weak investor sentiment,” and “tech stock volatility” as reasons to delay.
The right also personalizes the move through Sam Altman’s alleged “non-negotiable” valuation stance and broadens the stakes with Anthropic and security concerns, which are de-emphasized elsewhere.
All sides agree the IPO delay is under consideration, but they diverge on whether it reads as prudent timing or pressure from a shaky AI-market race.
summary: OpenAI is considering delaying its initial public offering until 2027, the New York Times reported on Thursday, citing three people involved in the company's internal deliberations.
CEO Sam Altman is reportedly pushing for a $1 trillion valuation, but advisers warned the company must accept a lower price or wait until 2027 for that target.
Advisers cautioned that recent volatility in tech stocks and SpaceX 's post-IPO performance could dampen retail investor enthusiasm for OpenAI's offering, the Times reported.
Shares of SoftBank Group, a major OpenAI investor, fell as much as 12% on Friday following the delay report, reversing earlier gains from IPO expectations.
OpenAI faces pressure to monetize amid $600 billion in projected hardware spending through 2030, while rival Anthropic pursues its own public debut in a crowded 2026 IPO pipeline.
title: OpenAI leans toward waiting until next year for IPO, NYT reports

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the episode as potential misconduct, leaning on phrases like “possibly violating federal law,” “caught on tape,” and “Tipping the scales” to cast Kennedy’s outreach as suspect, even as it ties the motive to helping Republicans keep control of Congress and exposes “mounting GOP anxiety.” Right-leaning coverage is more stripped down, stressing that he “urged” a candidate to “drop out,” with less attention to legal peril or White House “liaison” claims.
Center coverage sits between them, emphasizing the audio and a second candidate’s corroboration.
Both sides agree on the basic allegation, but diverge over whether it signals scandal, strategy, or simply hardball politics .
summary: On June 11, U.S. Secretary of Health and Human Services Robert F. Kennedy Jr. urged candidate Rick Stewart to withdraw from the Iowa 2nd Congressional District race, warning a Democratic House takeover would derail President Donald Trump's agenda.
Kennedy made a similar unrecorded appeal to Libertarian Marco Battaglia in Iowa's 3rd Congressional District on June 8, warning that the House could flip to Democrats if Battaglia stayed in the race.
Stewart interpreted the call as an attempt at quid pro quo, noting, "He was very careful about the words that he used, but the whole implication is: You help us, we'll help you."

Government ethics experts warned the recorded conversation may violate federal law, though the White House and Department of Health and Human Services did not answer questions regarding whether the calls were cleared by lawyers.

Republicans hold a slim congressional majority, and the White House is wary that a Democratic-controlled House could subpoena Kennedy over his health policy changes, with Kennedy stating, "I don't want to be fighting subpoenas.
title: Audio Shows RFK Jr. Urged Iowa Libertarian Candidates to Drop House Bid: Report

bias: Left-Leaning coverage casts the ruling as protection for “sick” and “disabled” immigrant children, stressing harm from “reporting” to “immigration authorities” and using courtroom language like “blocks” to imply a shield against state overreach.
Right-Leaning coverage is more procedural, framing the issue as the health department “sharing” or “halting” records and de-emphasizing the scale—hundreds of children—and the humanitarian stakes.

The sharpest dividing line is whether the law is portrayed as punitive enforcement or routine reporting, while both sides agree the order is temporary and tied to Tennessee’s new law, reflecting broader immigration and state-power tensions.
summary: On Wednesday, Davidson County Chancellor Patricia Head Moskal issued a temporary restraining order blocking the Tennessee Department of Health from reporting about 400 disabled immigrant children in the Children's Special Services program to immigration authorities.

State officials began sending letters June 1 warning families that Public Chapter 1106 requires reporting the immigration status of all benefit recipients to the Centralized Immigration Enforcement Division, which cooperates with U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement.

Three Nashville physicians sued to stop the policy, arguing it deters families from seeking necessary medical care; Michele Johnson of the Tennessee Justice Center called the requirement an "impossible choice for mothers."

The Tennessee Department of Health and physicians' attorneys are scheduled to argue the case at a July 2 hearing in Nashville, while the state attorney general's office has not commented on the lawsuit.

This measure is part of a broader legislative package Tennessee Republicans introduced this year to support President Donald Trump 's immigration crackdown, which requires government agencies to check legal status before providing public benefits.
title: Judge blocks Tennessee from reporting sick children to immigration authorities, for now

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Vance’s Nixon remarks as a “bizarre” and eyebrow-raising defense, using phrases like “going viral for all the wrong reasons” and spotlighting online mockery, while the right-leaning take strips away the controversy and recasts the appearance as a simple “LIVE NOW” event notice with timing details.

The biggest split is over substance: left and center stress Vance’s Watergate claim and the Nixon-Trump parallel, but only the center adds his “deep state” framing; the right de-emphasizes Watergate altogether.
Both sides share the Nixon context, yet their tone reveals a broader divide between scandal-focused skepticism and legacy-oriented normalization.
summary: On Thursday, Vice President JD Vance argued at the Richard Nixon Presidential Library in Yorba Linda, California, that the Watergate scandal would be a "12-hour news story" today, comparing "deep state" actions against President Richard Nixon to those against President Donald Trump.
Promoting his book, Communion, Vance expressed fascination with Nixon, stating the former president's historical legacy is "enjoying a bit of a renaissance" and that he has "always liked" him.

Vance claimed that if Watergate occurred tomorrow, it would be a "12-hour news story," arguing that the "same institutions" targeting Nixon also attempted to undermine Trump during his first administration.

Critics reacted sharply to the comments, with U.S. political editor Mikey Smith of The Mirror writing that "this is just not the thing you say in public," while historian Kevin M. Kruse remarked, "Oh, he's so close to getting it."

Nixon resigned in 1974 following the Watergate scandal, the only president to step down. New York attorney Ray Beckerman challenged the comparison, writing that "Nixon's corruption was infinitesimal compared to Trump's.
title: VP Vance, an Admirer of Richard Nixon, Says Watergate Would Be 'a 12-Hour News Story' Today

bias: Left-Leaning coverage casts Meta as trying to “silence” a former executive and spotlights the memoir’s “explosive” claims, including alleged “cruel” conduct and even a $50,000 penalty for criticism; right-leaning coverage shifts the lens to a free-speech and contract fight, using terms like “gag order,” “barring,” and “explosive memoir” to frame institutional pressure and legal overreach; center-leaning takes a similar conflict-heavy tone but is more explicit about Meta being “afraid” of what she said.

Across all perspectives, the shared core is the lawsuit itself, but the deeper divide is whether it reads as whistleblower suppression, corporate restraint, or a broader speech-versus-severance dispute.
summary: On Thursday, Sarah Wynn-Williams sued Meta in Northern California, seeking to invalidate a private arbitration order barring her from promoting her memoir, Careless People.

Meta obtained an emergency arbitration order in March 2025 after Careless People's publication, demanding Wynn-Williams cease disparaging comments and threatening fines of up to $50,000 per breach based on a 2017 severance agreement.

The lawsuit claims Meta representatives surveilled Wynn-Williams' public appearances to document compliance, while lawyers warned that speaking about the book could trigger fines, causing her to sit in silence at the Hay Festival.

Senator Chuck Grassley, the Republican chair of the Senate Judiciary Committee, wrote to CEO Mark Zuckerberg over the allegations, while Wynn-Williams separately filed a whistleblower complaint with the Securities and Exchange Commission regarding China.

Meta characterizes Wynn-Williams' 2017 departure as a firing for poor performance, while she maintains it was retaliation for reporting executive Joel Kaplan for sexual harassment—a characterization the company rejects.
title: Former Executive Sues Meta over Attempts to 'Silence' Her Memoir, 'Careless People'

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the bill as a defense against censorship, using protective language like “safeguarding” and the charged phrase “ book bans ,” while also stressing Massachusetts’ historic library tradition and its 2025 rank among states facing restriction attempts; right-leaning coverage mostly mirrors the bill’s core provisions but de-emphasizes that wider alarm, presenting the measure in a cooler, narrower policy register.
The sharpest dividing line is interpretive: one side treats the law as a civil-liberties shield, the other as a routine legislative move.
Both agree on the bipartisan vote, librarian authority, and age-appropriate standards, reflecting a shared baseline even as cultural battles over access and control shape the tone.
summary: The House passed a bipartisan bill earlier this month requiring school and public libraries to adopt formal policies for handling book challenges, protecting access to books by preventing removals based on personal or political beliefs.

Nearly 92% of complaints in 2025 were initiated by pressure groups, the report says, as Massachusetts ranked fourth nationally for restriction attempts with 4,235 unique titles challenged in the U.S. last year.

Rep. Christopher Markey, D-Dartmouth, noted that 39% of challenged titles focus on LGBTQ+ people or people of color, calling it "absolutely" important to keep them on shelves as librarians curate collections based on professional training.

Establishing a transparent system, the bill requires "clear and convincing evidence" before overruling a librarian's book selection, with Rep. Antonio F.D. Cabral, D-New Bedford, stating this creates fair procedures for handling complaints.

The bill now moves to a conference committee with the Senate to reconcile differences between chambers, as both versions allow parents to challenge materials deemed not age-appropriate, reflecting shared legislative intent.
title: Massachusetts House passes bill safeguarding libraries from book bans

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the dispute as another Trump-triggered culture clash, stressing the estate’s “slams” and “decries” while adding a jab that he likely hasn’t listened to the lyrics; center coverage stays more procedural, emphasizing “without permission,” the “long-term feud,” and the prior announcement; right-leaning coverage softens conflict by foregrounding the “Freedom 250 kickoff celebration” and the estate’s quick objection while naming Christopher Macchio prominently.
The dividing line is whether the event is portrayed as a patriotic commemoration or a Trump controversy.
All sides agree the estate objected and the song was still performed, reflecting broader ideological splits over Trump, cultural symbolism, and artistic consent.
summary: The estate of the late Leonard Cohen has criticized President Donald Trump for using Cohen's song 'Hallelujah' at the recent Freedom 250 rally.
The estate stated on Cohen's Instagram account that 'this use is not authorized, and the Estate does not support or approve of this or any similar usage.'
Several other artists have also voiced opposition to Trump using their music, including Neil Young and The Rolling Stones.
title: Leonard Cohen’s estate slams Trump for use of ‘Hallelujah’ at Freedom 250 Rally

bias: Left-Leaning coverage treats the 6.49% mortgage rate as a minor move, stressing “little changed” and “edged higher” to downplay drama; the center briefly adds household impact, noting rates can “add hundreds of dollars” and cut “purchasing power.” Right-leaning coverage shifts the frame outward, using “Iran,” “inflation uncertainty,” and “ housing affordability unlikely” to portray the uptick as part of a broader economic and geopolitical squeeze.
The key dividing lines are causation and consequence: raw rate data versus external threats and long-term affordability.
All sides agree the increase was small, but they diverge on whether it is mostly a stable weekly update or a warning sign.
summary: Freddie Mac reported Thursday that the average 30-year fixed Mortgage rate rose to 6.49% from 6.47% last week, continuing elevated Borrowing costs for prospective homebuyers.
Ongoing conflict with Iran has disrupted Persian Gulf Oil flows since late February, driving up inflation and Bond yields, which pushed Mortgage Rates higher.
The 10-year Treasury yield stood at 4.38% midday Thursday on the bond market; Borrowing costs on 15-year fixed-rate Mortgage loans also rose this week.
Uncertainty about Rates trajectory has kept many homebuyers on the sidelines, while existing home Sales hover near a 4-million annual pace, far below the 5.2-million historic norm.
The Federal Reserve has signaled potential interest rate hikes later this year, though Oil prices recently decreased amid negotiations to end the war with Iran.
title: Average 30-year U.S. mortgage rate rises to 6.49%, little changed from its range the past 6 weeks

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the rent freeze as a tenant win and a promise kept, stressing “historic victory,” affordability, and even evidence on rising building costs, while right-leaning coverage pivots to suspicion, using phrases like “absolute farce,” “stacking board,” and “controlled” to suggest a predetermined, quasi-rigged process.
The pivotal divide is whether the resignation and mayoral influence signal accountability or impropriety.

Both sides note the freeze on about 1 million apartments and Mamdani’s campaign pledge, but left outlets celebrate policy delivery, while right outlets emphasize legitimacy concerns and landlord outrage, reflecting broader conflicts over housing intervention and political power.
summary: Christina Smyth resigned from the NYC Rent Guidelines Board before a vote on a Mayor Mamdani-promised rent freeze, citing political bias and predetermined political outcomes rather than objective fact-finding.
The Rent Guidelines Board, mostly appointed by Mayor Mamdani, is expected to approve the first-ever two-year rent freeze affecting over one million rent-stabilized apartments starting October 2026.

Landlord groups expressed concern over Smyth's resignation and the board's reduced landlord representation, fearing legal challenges due to alleged political influence, while tenant advocates support the freeze citing affordability pressures.
The board considers various financial data and public testimony, including arguments about rising landlord costs and landlord profits, in deciding to implement the rent freeze.
title: New York City freezes rents for one million apartments in Mayor Mamdani victory
topic_full: New York State Politics · New York

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the deadlock around the fire ’s catastrophic stakes, stressing the “deadly” and “devastating” impact and the possibility of a mistrial, while right-leaning coverage turns the same courtroom pause into a more accusatory, dramatic saga, using terms like “bizarre,” “strange twist,” “firebug,” and “last-ditch effort” to spotlight uncertainty and culpability.
The biggest dividing line is the jury’s reversal from seeming verdict to deadlock: left de-emphasizes that twist, whereas center and right highlight it as unusual.
Both sides agree on the standstill, the accused’s role, and Friday’s return, but they diverge sharply over whether the story is mainly about disaster, doubt, or blame.
summary: On Thursday, June 25, 2026, a federal jury reached a verdict in the trial of Jonathan Rinderknecht, accused of starting the 2025 Palisades Fire . The court will announce the decision shortly.

Prosecutors argued that Rinderknecht intentionally started the Lachman Fire on January 1, 2025, which smoldered underground before erupting into the Palisades Fire, killing 12 people and destroying over 5,400 homes.

While prosecutors placed Rinderknecht 30 feet from the flames using cellphone data, defense attorney Steve Haney argued the government lacked physical evidence, noting his client called 911 multiple times to report the fire.
If convicted on three federal charges including destruction of property by fire and arson affecting property used in interstate commerce, Rinderknecht faces a maximum of 45 years in federal prison.
The 2025 disaster burned more than 23,000 acres of land across the Pacific Palisades, Malibu, and Santa Monica mountains, marking one of the third most destructive incidents in California history.
title: A surprise deadlocked jury in the Palisades Fire trial leaves attorneys weighing next steps

bias: Left-Leaning coverage foregrounds the racial-justice stakes, using phrases like “majority-Black” and “dilute Black voting power” to frame the ruling as a setback for representation, while right-leaning coverage strips the story down to a legal defeat, emphasizing the judge “rules against” the effort and the lack of evidence.
The center adds the most legal texture, citing Section 2 and “racial gerrymandering,” but stays less interpretive.
The key dividing line is whether the case is seen as protecting Black voting power or resisting race-based districting; all sides agree the court rejected the claim and found insufficient proof.
summary: U.S. District Judge Glen H. Davidson ruled that plaintiffs did not prove their claims that the 2022 DeSoto County district maps were drawn to dilute Black voting power.
The lawsuit filed in 2024 alleged that the DeSoto County electoral map diluted Black voting power in county elections.
The ACLU of Mississippi described the ruling as deeply disappointing.
The case was noted by the state Republican chairman as reflecting Democrats' frustration over election losses in DeSoto County.
title: Judge rules against effort to create majority-Black DeSoto County districts

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the story as a sweeping institutional failure, stressing “staggering amounts,” “watched and took no action,” and the claim that agents “allowed” fentanyl onto streets, while right-leaning coverage sharpens the partisan edge, recasting it as a Biden-era scandal and reaching for loaded shorthand like “Fast and Furious 2.0” and “surprise you.” The biggest dividing lines are whether the operation is explained as a law-enforcement strategy to “build bigger/stronger cases” or as negligence bordering on complicity, and whether accountability is centered on the DEA itself or on broader political blame.
Both sides converge on danger, oversight, and the watchdog probe, but they split along law-and-order skepticism versus partisan governance critique.
summary: Following an Associated Press investigation, the Drug Enforcement Administration requested an independent review from the Justice Department inspector general of its actions allowing fentanyl shipments to circulate in New Mexico.
From 2023 to 2025, the DEA allowed hundreds of thousands of fentanyl pills to hit New Mexico streets to build larger prosecutions, despite warnings that 2 milligrams of the drug can kill an adult.

In one June 2023 case in Albuquerque, more than a dozen agents monitored a shipment of 74,000 pills using Title III wiretaps and aerial surveillance while allowing the drugs to reach their destination.
A 19-year veteran DEA agent spoke out after witnessing the agency 'walk' 100,000 fentanyl pills, saying he could no longer countenance allowing illicit drugs to reach communities.
Separately, New Mexico's governor announced a state investigation, while the DEA maintained to the Associated Press that its agents' actions were lawful and consistent with department guidance.
title: DEA asks watchdog to investigate claims that agents permitted fentanyl to hit the streets

bias: Left-Leaning coverage is de-emphasized here, while the center frames Katz’s remarks as a narrow security statement, stressing “security zones,” “no timetable,” and even “jihadist threats” to justify an indefinite stay; the right expands this into a harder-edged sovereignty story, using words like “remain,” “indefinitely,” “occupied,” and “vows” to portray resolve, permanence, and a refusal of withdrawal.

A key dividing line is whether the move is presented as defensive necessity or as entrenched military control, with the right also elevating Netanyahu and even Tehran’s warning to amplify regional stakes.
Both sides converge on the core fact of no withdrawal deadline, but part ways over how politically charged—and how enduring—that decision is.
summary: On Friday, Israel confirmed it has no timetable for withdrawing forces from Lebanon, Gaza, and Syria, as US-brokered talks continue in Washington aimed at ending the wider Middle East conflict.

To protect northern communities from Hezbollah attacks, Israel maintains a buffer zone about 10 km into Lebanon, with officials emphasizing that any redeployment of IDF forces depends on the region's complete demilitarization.

Defence Minister Israel Katz stated troops must remain in the security zone to defend residents against "jihadist elements," as the military reported killing suspected Hezbollah operatives on Thursday in southern Lebanon.

Government spokesman David Mencer denied reports of any Israeli pullback, asserting forces will not withdraw until Hezbollah is disarmed, while a senior Lebanese military official noted developments "show the opposite of a pullback."

US Secretary of State Marco Rubio said the neighbors are nearing a "commitment of intent" during negotiations, though Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu insists troops will remain in south Lebanon "as long as necessary.
title: Israel: No Troop Withdrawal Timeframe From Lebanon, Gaza

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Netanyahu and Trump as political betrayers, using loaded phrases like “misleads,” “sells out,” and “reverting to type” to cast the Lebanon ceasefire as a cynical sellout, while right-leaning coverage stresses security and resolve, spotlighting “no restrictions,” “freedom of action,” and “great progress” to portray Israel as constrained only by hostile pressure.

The sharpest divide is over whether the story is mainly diplomatic betrayal or a legitimate response to Hezbollah threats; the right also elevates JD Vance’s remarks and “terror group” language, while the left de-emphasizes them.
Both sides agree on Netanyahu’s statement, but interpret its geopolitical meaning through starkly different ideological lenses.
summary: On Monday, Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu insisted that troops in southern Lebanon retain "full freedom of action" against "direct or emerging threats," reaffirming commitment to the security zone.
Negotiators from the U.S. and Iran established a "deconfliction mechanism" to oversee military operations in Lebanon, though the high-level committee currently excludes Israel.

Yet The New York Times reported the IDF issued directives limiting soldiers to defensive actions against "imminent threats," contrasting Netanyahu's claim of "no restrictions," which Local outlet Channel 12 noted.

Vice President JD Vance defended President Donald Trump's threats to Iran while confirming Switzerland-based negotiations aim to end the conflict within 60 days and require Iran to "rein in" Hezbollah.

Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent issued a temporary 60-day license authorizing Iranian oil sales as part of the framework, which also permits International Atomic Energy Agency inspectors to verify Iran's nuclear material use.
title: Netanyahu: ‘No restrictions’ on Israel military acting to thwart Hezbollah threat

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the story as a human-rights controversy, centering the exhibit on “displaced Palestinians” and treating the resignation as a protest against a potentially unfair museum narrative, while right-leaning coverage sharpens the conflict with terms like “one-sided,” “protest,” “quits,” and especially “lone Jewish trustee,” turning the resignation into a broader accusation of institutional bias and “anti-Zionism.” The key dividing lines are Berlin’s identity, the Nakba label, and warnings about “anti-Jewish hatred”: left coverage de-emphasizes those details, center coverage notes historical context and possible harm, and right coverage foregrounds them as proof of ideological capture.
Both sides agree the resignation stemmed from the exhibit, but diverge over whether it signals justice concerns or cultural-political grievance.
summary: On Monday, Canadian Museum for Human Rights trustee Mark Berlin resigned, citing concerns that a forthcoming exhibit about the Palestinian Nakba lacks vital historical context and could incite hostility against Jewish people.

Opening this Saturday, the exhibit focuses on the displacement of around 750,000 Palestinians between 1947 and 1949, though Berlin contends it fails to include the simultaneous expulsion of roughly 850,000 Jews from Arab states.

In his resignation letter to Heritage Minister Marc Miller, Berlin argued the museum rebuffed Jewish community consultation requests for over two years, claiming the institution replaced trust with ideology.
Museum chief executive Isha Khan said last week the exhibit is a "modest-sized" display not intended to cover the entire history of the region, with curatorial decisions remaining with the museum.

Jewish organizations have raised similar concerns regarding antisemitism, though Khan stated the museum has added programming on antisemitism and committed to featuring stories about Jewish displacement in future exhibits.
title: Human rights museum board trustee resigns over exhibit on displaced Palestinians

bias: Left-Leaning coverage treats the episode as a playful, media-critical squabble, emphasizing Vance “pokes fun” and “clapped back” while still noting the broader “pregnancy fashion debate” and even the phrase “pro-natalist movement.” Right-leaning coverage turns it into a clash with elite media, using sharper terms like “mocks,” “fires back,” “weaponizing,” and “political spin” to frame the Times as overreaching and Vance’s receipt as a triumph.
The key dividing line is whether the $8.75 dress signals symbolism or simple budget shopping.
Both sides share the humor-and-receipt angle, but left-right tension tracks a deeper divide over media power and MAGA identity.
summary: On Wednesday, Second Lady Usha Vance posted an image of her receipt for an $8.75 coral maternity dress from Old Navy on X, responding to a New York Times article about pregnancy aesthetics among high-profile women in President Donald Trump's Maga movement.

Chief Fashion Critic Vanessa Friedman wrote in The Times that the simultaneous pregnancies of high-profile women close to President Trump, including Vance, offer an idealized image of womanhood aligning with the pronatalist movement's vision for society.

Sharing her receipt, Vance wrote, "Now that we know the political significance of my $8.75 coral maternity dress from Old Navy, can't wait to hear what the NYT has to say about my elastic-waistband pants and compression socks!"

The coral dress, originally priced at $50 but reduced to $7.47, is now sold out in all sizes, while social media commenters praised the Second Lady with one writing, "Beautiful and frugal and relatable!"

Friedman noted that White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt and Katie Miller, wife of Deputy Chief of Staff Stephen Miller, were also pregnant this year, reflecting the administration's pronatalist emphasis on motherhood.
title: Usha Vance Shares Receipt of $8 Dress After NYT Report on Maga Pregnancies
California Lawmakers Pass ACA 20 to Double Rainy Day Fund Cap

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the new AI workforce group as a defensive answer to looming harm, stressing “potential job losses,” “displaced workers,” and even “catastrophic job losses,” while warning America is “rushing” ahead “without much of a plan.” By contrast, the center leans managerial and reassuring, emphasizing “transition,” “development,” a “people strategy,” and the practical logistics of training and job placement.

Both note the bipartisan launch, the Holcomb-Raimondo pairing, and about $500 million in backing, but the left reads those figures as evidence of urgent protection, while the center presents them as organized policy capacity.
summary: On Thursday, former Republican Indiana Gov. Eric Holcomb and former Democratic Rhode Island Gov. Gina Raimondo launched RAISE US, a workforce initiative aimed at preparing American workers for an economy increasingly centered on artificial intelligence.

The organization aims to mitigate labor market anxieties by implementing a bipartisan "people strategy," with Raimondo warning that failing to support workers during the AI transition could trigger an "automated decline."

Backed by more than $500 million in corporate and philanthropic commitments, RAISE US is launching pilot programs in Arkansas, Connecticut, Maryland, and Utah to develop "concrete programs" for job transitions.

Advisory members include AFL-CIO president Liz Schuler and leaders from major corporations like Amazon, Microsoft, and OpenAI, who are collaborating to ensure training systems keep pace with technological advancements.

Organizers pledge to raise an additional $500 million soon while designing new apprenticeship models and corporate incentives to establish national standards for worker support in an AI-driven future.
title: Former Indiana Gov. Eric Holcomb helps launch national organization centered on impact of AI on economy

bias: Left-Leaning coverage leans into urgency and empathy, using phrases like “rush to send aid,” “victims,” and “devastating earthquakes” to spotlight anxious diaspora families and relief efforts, even comparing the quakes to Haiti 2010; right-leaning coverage shifts toward harsher scale and logistics, with terms like “deadly,” “damaged,” and “thousands missing” framing the same crisis as a more severe, infrastructure-strained emergency.

The airport detail becomes a key divider: one side stresses it as “closed,” the other as “damaged,” while both still converge on diaspora-led donations, medicines, and community coordination as the shared response.
summary: On Thursday, Venezuelans across the United States launched donation drives following 7.2- and 7.5-magnitude earthquakes that struck Venezuela Wednesday night, killing at least 188 people.
The quakes, among the strongest in Venezuela in more than a century, trapped residents in mountain communities like Caribia and destroyed homes, leaving families in the U.S. scrambling for updates.

Communities in Doral, Florida, and Katy, Texas—nicknamed "Katyzuela"—rallied to collect money, medicine, and water, while Doral-based Global Empowerment Mission began packing medical supplies for shipment.
The U.S. government pledged $150 million for relief efforts, as the State Department coordinated search and rescue teams from Los Angeles and Fairfax County, Virginia.

Despite damaged infrastructure, Global Empowerment Mission's Billy Richardson, U.S. logistics director, expects no major delays in aid delivery, while maritime industry consultant Daniel Arenas noted the challenge of managing resources.
title: Venezuelans in the US rush to send aid to earthquake victims, but Caracas airport is closed

bias: Left-Leaning coverage leans into moralized, sensational framing, using phrases like “learns his fate” and “alleged jealous rage” to foreground punishment and emotional fallout, while right-leaning coverage sharpens the law-and-order angle with “tried to kill” and “behind bars,” presenting the sentence as a stark reckoning.

The key dividing line is motive and legal context: the center is de-emphasized in this request’s main dynamic but adds names, charges, a guilty plea, and the gasoline detail, while left and right strip that context away.
Both sides agree on the 40-year sentence and the brutal attack, reflecting shared emphasis on violence and accountability within broader cultural divides.
summary: On Thursday, Circuit Judge James Reynolds sentenced Shotsie Michael Buck-Hayes to 40 years in prison for the July 30, 2025, attack on Danville City Councilman Lee Vogler.
Buck-Hayes doused Vogler in gasoline at Showcase Magazine, claiming the attack stemmed from an alleged affair between the councilman and his wife.

Vogler suffered second and third-degree burns to 60 percent of his body and spent months in the ICU recovering, while his wife, Tiffany Blair Vogler, told the court their family's lives "were changed forever."
Buck-Hayes pleaded guilty in April to malicious wounding and attempted first-degree murder, receiving a life sentence with 35 years to serve plus five years for the attempted murder charge.
Reflecting on the past year, Vogler told 10 News he intends to spend his remaining life on the "highest mountain," grateful to have returned to his council duties.
title: The man who set a Virginia city council member on fire gets 40 years in prison

bias: Left-Leaning coverage treats the story as a fun sports-cultural crossover, stressing the White Sox link, the 2005 World Series ball, and phrases like “cool moment,” “special gift,” and “incredible honor,” while the right shifts the focus to reverent papal significance, using uplifted language such as “speaks to the heart,” “the world is listening,” and “Christian humanism.” The sharp dividing line is whether the baseball anecdote is the point or merely a vehicle for broader religious meaning; the right also re-centers the event through a “private audience” and a different presenter, while the left foregrounds Pierzynski and the game-used memorabilia.
Both sides agree on the Vatican setting and the baseball connection, but they frame the moment through distinct cultural lenses.
summary: On August 11, the Chicago White Sox will honor Pope Leo XIV during their game against Cincinnati, distributing pope-themed hats to fans.
Pope Leo XIV, formerly known as Robert Prevost, attended the 2005 World Series opener in Chicago 21 years ago, a moment now commemorated with a graphic installation at Rate Field.
Pierzynski recently presented the pontiff with the baseball from the final out of Game 1, sharing photos of the Vatican exchange on social media on Thursday.
Since his election last May, the first U.S. pope has received multiple sports gifts, including a Chicago Bears jersey from Vice President Vance and a Chicago Bulls jersey from Chuck Swirsky.
His collection reflects deep ties to Chicago, where he grew up; he also possesses a bat once belonging to Hall of Famer Nellie Fox and a signed jersey from Paul Konerko.
title: Pope Leo XIV receives World Series baseball from former White Sox catcher A.J. Pierzynski

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames FIFA’s decision as a win for LGBTQ + visibility, foregrounding “Pride” and “Rainbow Flags” as symbols of inclusion, while right-leaning coverage sharpens the conflict with phrases like “Despite Protests” and “anti-LGBTQ+ nations,” turning the match into a culture-war flashpoint.

The center takes a different tack: its “Truth or Fake” style and “green light” wording stress verification and add the Pride-weekend scheduling quirk, treating context as explanatory rather than combative.
All sides agree fans may bring flags, but they diverge on whether that reads as rights, controversy, or simply event logistics.
summary: FIFA confirmed Thursday that fans may bring rainbow flags to Friday's World Cup match between Egypt and Iran at Lumen Field in Seattle, rejecting both nations' federation objections to LGBTQ+ symbols.

Seattle's local World Cup organizing committee branded Friday's game a "Pride Match" earlier this year to coincide with annual celebrations, prompting objections from the Egyptian and Iranian football federations citing cultural values.

FIFA President Gianni Infantino told Swiss magazine Die Weltwoche in January that "there will be no 'Pride Match' at the World Cup," emphasizing celebrations are organized by external groups, not FIFA.
This marks a shift from the 2022 World Cup in Qatar, where FIFA threatened yellow cards against captains planning to wear "OneLove" armbands, citing rules against political messaging.

Friday's match is critical for both teams' tournament advancement, while human rights groups note Iran and Egypt impose severe penalties on LGBTQ+ individuals, including potential imprisonment and fines.
title: World Cup fans to be allowed to bring rainbow flags for Egypt-Iran match

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the ruling as a protective win, spotlighting how the judge “blocks” a Trump “overhaul” and stressing fears that the rule would “dangerously shrink” nursing and other healthcare pipelines; right-leaning coverage keeps the same court action but recasts it as a procedural check on a Trump “plan,” emphasizing that Congress’s broader borrowing caps still remain and softening the sense of total defeat.
The sharpest dividing lines are the healthcare workforce impact, the narrowing of “professional” degrees, and whether the case is about harm or restraint.
Both sides agree the rule was blocked, but they diverge over whether that signals safeguarding access or limiting executive power.
summary: A federal judge blocked the Trump administration's new rule lowering federal student loan limits for graduate degrees in nursing and healthcare fields.
Eight trade organizations sued to stop the rule from taking effect on July 1, and the judge sided with them.
Judge Beryl Howell ruled that the Education Department lacked authority to narrow the definition of 'professional degree,' violating the Administrative Procedure Act.
The judge did not prevent enforcement of the new loan caps, stating she could not override Congress's elimination of uncapped borrowing.
title: Judge blocks new professional student loan restrictions
